q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The total number of geometrical isomers shown by \(\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}\)complex ion is three.
Reason (R): \(\quad\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}\)complex ion has an octahedral geometry.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The total number of geometrical isomers shown by \(\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}\)complex ion is three.
Reason (R): \(\quad\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}\)complex ion has an octahedral geometry.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
solution: $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}$has octahedral geometry with two geometrical isomers.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The Spin only magnetic moment value of square planar complex PtNH32ClNH2CH3Cl is _______ B.M. (Nearest integer)
( Given atomic number for Pt=78)
question:
The Spin only magnetic moment value of square planar complex PtNH32ClNH2CH3Cl is _______ B.M. (Nearest integer)
( Given atomic number for Pt=78)
solution:
Spin only magnetic moment can be calculated by the following formula
Magnetic moment =n(n+2)
Where, n is number of unpaired electrons.

In the given compound, Pt2+d8

Pt2+→dsp2 hybridization and have no unpaired electrons..

∴ Magnetic moment

 =n(n+2)=0(0+2)=0

subject: Chemistry
q: Homoleptic octahedral complexes of a metal ion <math><mi>′</mi><msup><mrow><mi>M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mi>′</mi></math> with three monodentate ligands <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> absorb wavelengths in the region of green, blue and red respectively. The increasing order of the ligand strength is:
question: Homoleptic octahedral complexes of a metal ion <math><mi>′</mi><msup><mrow><mi>M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mi>′</mi></math> with three monodentate ligands <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> absorb wavelengths in the region of green, blue and red respectively. The increasing order of the ligand strength is:
solution:
Observed order of wavelengths, λabs =L3>L1>L2.

(Red light has highest wavelength of 700 nm while blue is the shortest with a wavelength of about 480 nm)

So, ∆0 (strength of ligands) order  will be L2>L1>L3.

(As ∆0 α 1λabs )

Hence, order will be L2>L1>L3.

Therefore, Strength of ligand ∝∆Absorption.

q:
The spin-only magnetic moment value of $\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n+-}}$ ion formed among $\mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Zn} \mathrm{Mn}$ and Cu that has the least enthalpy of atomisation is ________. (in nearest integer)
Here n is equal to the number of diamagnetic complexes among $\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right],\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$,
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \text { and }\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{I}\right]$
question:
The spin-only magnetic moment value of $\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n+-}}$ ion formed among $\mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Zn} \mathrm{Mn}$ and Cu that has the least enthalpy of atomisation is ________. (in nearest integer)
Here n is equal to the number of diamagnetic complexes among $\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right],\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$,
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \text { and }\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{I}\right]$
solution:
$\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3$, Paramagnetic
$\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2$, Diamagnetic
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3$, Paramagnetic
$\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{I}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3$, Diamagnetic
Hence the value of $n$ is 2
Least value of enthalpy of atomisation among Ni ,
$\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Mn}$ and Cu is of Zn
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Zn}^{+2}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} \\
& \mu=0
\end{aligned}$

question: The correct combination is :
solution:
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ is square planar, diamagnetic $(0$ unpaired electrons) with $d s p^2$ hybridisation. 
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ - is tetrahedral,diamagnetic ( $0$ unpaired electrons) with $s p^3$ hybridisation. 
$\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ is tetrahedral, paramagnetic ($2$ unpaired electrons) with $s p^3$ hybridisation. 
Hence, the option (c) is the correct answer.

subject: Chemistry
q:
A chromium complex with a formula $\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has a spin only magnetic moment value of 3.87 BM and its solution conductivity corresponds to $1: 2$ electrolyte. 2.75 g of the complex solution was initially passed through a cation exchanger. The solution obtained after the process was reacted with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. The amount of AgCl formed in the above process is $\_\_\_\_$ g. (Nearest integer)
[Given: Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Cr}: 52 ; \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5, \mathrm{Ag}: 108, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{H}: 1$]
question:
A chromium complex with a formula $\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has a spin only magnetic moment value of 3.87 BM and its solution conductivity corresponds to $1: 2$ electrolyte. 2.75 g of the complex solution was initially passed through a cation exchanger. The solution obtained after the process was reacted with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. The amount of AgCl formed in the above process is $\_\_\_\_$ g. (Nearest integer)
[Given: Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Cr}: 52 ; \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5, \mathrm{Ag}: 108, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{H}: 1$]
solution:
The complex $\text{CrCl}_3 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ with magnetic moment 3.87 BM indicates $3.87 = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$ giving n = 3 unpaired electrons, confirming Cr³⁺.
A 1:2 electrolyte means structure is $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O})_5\text{Cl}]^{2+}\text{Cl}_2^-$.
Molar mass = 52 + 5(18) + 35.5 + 6(18) = 285.5 g/mol.
Moles of complex = 2.75/285.5 = 0.00963 mol.
After cation exchange, 2 Cl⁻ remain per formula unit (from the 1:2 electrolyte), giving 0.00963 × 2 = 0.01926 mol of free Cl⁻.
Reacting with excess AgNO₃: mass of AgCl = 0.01926 × 143.5 = 2.76 g ≈ 3 g (nearest integer).

q: Spin only magnetic moment in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>BM</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo></msup></math> is :
question: Spin only magnetic moment in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>BM</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo></msup></math> is :
solution:
In Fe(CO)4C2O4+

Oxidation no. of Fe=+3 

26Fe+3=Ar183 d54 s0 

CO is a Strong field ligand, so paring occurs against the Hund's rule. 

Fe+3=t2g2,2,1 eg0,0 

Number of unpaired electron =1 

so magnetic moment μ=nn+2 B.M. =11+2=3=1.73 BM

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct order of the following complexes in terms of their crystal field stabilization energies is :
question: The correct order of the following complexes in terms of their crystal field stabilization energies is :
solution:
Crystal field splitting energy $(\Delta) \propto$ charge or oxidation state of central metal atom.

Crystal field splitting energy $(\Delta) \propto$ Field strength of ligand (and chelation)
Crystal field stabilisation energy (CFSE) $=\left[-0.4 \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{g}_g}\right.$ +0.6 eg] $\Delta_{\circ}$ (for octahedral)
For,
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_3\right]^{3+}: \mathrm{Co}^{3+}: \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^6 \mathrm{eg}^0 ; \mathrm{CFSE}=-2.4\left(\Delta_0\right)_1} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}: \mathrm{Co}^{3+}: \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^6 \mathrm{eg}^0 ; \mathrm{CFSE}=-2.4\left(\Delta_0\right)_2} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{2+}: \mathrm{Co}^{2+}: \mathrm{t}_{2 g}^5 \mathrm{eg}^2 ; \mathrm{CFSE}=-0.8\left(\Delta_0\right)_3} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+}: \mathrm{Co}^{2+}: \mathrm{e}^4 \mathrm{t}_2^3 ; \mathrm{CFSE}=-1.2 \Delta_{\mathrm{t}}} \\ & \left(\mathrm{as}:\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{t}}\right)=\frac{4}{9}\left(\Delta_0\right)_3\right) \\ & \because \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{t}} < \left(\Delta_0\right)_3 < \left(\Delta_0\right)_2 < \left(\Delta_0\right)_1\end{aligned}$

q: Total number of moles of AgCl precipitated on addition of excess of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> to one mole each of the following complexes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi>Cl</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Ni</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Pt</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Pd</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is
question: Total number of moles of AgCl precipitated on addition of excess of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> to one mole each of the following complexes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi>Cl</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Ni</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Pt</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Pd</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is
solution:
Silver nitrate on reaction of chloride ion gives silver chloride as precipitate. Chloride ions outside of coordination sphere will only give precipitates. Thus,
CoNH34Cl2Cl⇒ Gives 1 mole AgCl
NiH2O6Cl2⇒ Gives 2 moles AgCl
PtNH32Cl2⇒ Gives No AgCl
PdNH34Cl2⇒ Gives 2 moles AgCl
Total number of moles of AgCl=5 mole.

Among the given complexes, number of paramagnetic complexes is
question:
FeCN64-

FeCN63-

TiCN63-

NiCN42-

CoCN63-

Among the given complexes, number of paramagnetic complexes is
solution:
Complexes in which central atom has unpaired electrons is said to be paramagnetic in nature and where unpaired electrons are not present are diamagnetic in nature.

FeCN63- ,   Fe+3  3d5 → t2g2,2  eg1, paramagnetic (one unpaired electron)

FeCN64-,  Fe+2 3d6→t2g6eg0 ,diamagnetic 

TiCN63-,  Ti+3(3d1)→t2g1 , Paramagnetic (1 unpaired electron)

NiCN42-, Ni+2(3d8)→ Diamagnetic

CoCN63-,   Co+3(3d6)→t2g6, diamagnetic

question: The crystal field stabilization energy (CFSE) of <math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math>, respectively, are:
solution:

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List I with List II:

List I
(Complexes)		 List II
(Hybridisation)
(A) Ni(CO)4 I sp3
(B) CuNH342+ II dsp2
(C) FeNH362+ III sp3d2
(D) FeH2O62+ IV d2sp3
question:
Match List I with List II:

List I
(Complexes)		 List II
(Hybridisation)
(A) Ni(CO)4 I sp3
(B) CuNH342+ II dsp2
(C) FeNH362+ III sp3d2
(D) FeH2O62+ IV d2sp3
options: A – II, B – I, C – III, D – IV
A – I, B – II, C – III, D – IV
A – II, B – I, C – IV, D – III
A – I, B– II, C – IV, D – III
solution:
Ni is in zero oxidation state in [Ni(CO)4]. So, the electronic configuration of Ni is 3d8 4s2. As CO is a strong ligand, it pushes all the electrons in the 3d orbital, therefore the hybridisation of [Ni(CO)4] is sp3 and it has tetrahedral geometry. It is diamagnetic due to the absence of unpaired electrons.

CuNH342+ ion, oxidation state of Cu is +2 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d9.  So, there would be a rearrangement of electrons in Cu2+ because of the NH3 strong field ligand . And the last electron in the d-orbital would be out waiting for the N's electrons to fill up first. So, the 4 electron pairs from N would be in one 3d, one 4s, & two 4p orbitals and in the third place of 4p orbital, the e- from 3d would take place. So, you can say the hybridisation here would be dsp2.

FeNH362+ ion, oxidation state of Fe is +2 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d6. NH3 is strong field ligand with complex. So, no pairing will be there. So, you can say the hybridisation here would be d2sp3.

 

FeH2O62+ ion, oxidation state of Fe is +2 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d6. There are 4 unpaired electrons in 3d orbital.  So, you can say the hybridisation here would be sp3d2.

So, correct order of matching is given in the below table:

List I
(Complexes)		 List II
(Hybridisation)
Ni(CO)4 sp3
CuNH342+ dsp2
FeNH362+ d2sp3
FeH2O62+ sp3d2

If 'M' and 'C' do not absorb light and only 'L' absorbs then the titration plot between absorbed light <math><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus volume of ligand 'L' <math><mo>(</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>)</mo></math> would look like:
question: In a complexometric titration of metal ion with ligand <math><mi>M</mi><mi> </mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>→</mo><mi>C</mi><mi> </mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> end point is estimated spectrophotometrically (through light absorption). If 'M' and 'C' do not absorb light and only 'L' absorbs then the titration plot between absorbed light <math><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus volume of ligand 'L' <math><mo>(</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>)</mo></math> would look like:
solution: Initially ligand consumed by metal due to formation of complex. So absorbed light  <math><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>)</mo></math>  remain constant, after complex formation is completed, extra volume of ligand solution increases ligand concentration and also increases absorbed light.

subject: Chemistry
q:
$\mathrm{CrCl}_3 \cdot \mathrm{xNH}_3$ can exist as a complex. 0.1 molal aqueous solution of this complex shows a depression in freezing point of $0.558^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assuming $100 \%$ ionisation of this complex and coordination number of Cr is 6 , the complex will be
(Given $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
question:
$\mathrm{CrCl}_3 \cdot \mathrm{xNH}_3$ can exist as a complex. 0.1 molal aqueous solution of this complex shows a depression in freezing point of $0.558^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assuming $100 \%$ ionisation of this complex and coordination number of Cr is 6 , the complex will be
(Given $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \Delta T_f=i K_f m \\ & 0.558=i \times 1.86 \times 0.1 \\ & i=\frac{0.558}{0.186}=3\end{aligned}$
Number of ions when 100\% ionisation takes place $=3$
$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text { aqueous solution }}\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}^{2+}\right]^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
Number of ions $=3$

q:
The correct statements among I to III are:
(I) Valence bond theory cannot explain the color exhibited by transition metal complexes.
(II) Valence bond theory can predict quantitatively the magnetic properties of transition metal complexes.
(III) Valence bond theory cannot distinguish ligands as weak and strong field ones.
question:
The correct statements among I to III are:
(I) Valence bond theory cannot explain the color exhibited by transition metal complexes.
(II) Valence bond theory can predict quantitatively the magnetic properties of transition metal complexes.
(III) Valence bond theory cannot distinguish ligands as weak and strong field ones.
solution: Valence Bond Theory cannot explain the colour of transition metal complexes .this theory was also unable to distinguish between weak and strong Fields ligands. Valence Bond Theory does not explain and give quantitative interpretation of magnetic data. Hence, statement (II) is not right.

subject: Chemistry
q:
$5.33$ g of $CrCl_3 \cdot 6H_2O$, which is a $1 : 3$ electrolyte, is dissolved in water and is passed through a cation exchanger. The chloride ions in the eluted solution, on treatment with $AgNO_3$ results in $8.61$ g of $AgCl$. The ratio of moles of complex reacted and moles of $AgCl$ formed is _______ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
[Molar mass in g mol$^{-1}$ Cr : $52$, Ag : $108$, Cl : $35.5$, H : $1$, O : $16$]
question:
$5.33$ g of $CrCl_3 \cdot 6H_2O$, which is a $1 : 3$ electrolyte, is dissolved in water and is passed through a cation exchanger. The chloride ions in the eluted solution, on treatment with $AgNO_3$ results in $8.61$ g of $AgCl$. The ratio of moles of complex reacted and moles of $AgCl$ formed is _______ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
[Molar mass in g mol$^{-1}$ Cr : $52$, Ag : $108$, Cl : $35.5$, H : $1$, O : $16$]
solution:
Molar mass of $CrCl_3 \cdot 6H_2O = 52 + 3 \times 35.5 + 6 \times 18 = 266.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

Moles of complex reacted $= \dfrac{5.33}{266.5} = 0.02 \text{ mol}$

Since the complex is a $1:3$ electrolyte, it dissociates to give one complex cation and three chloride anions. Its formula is $[Cr(H_2O)_6]Cl_3$.

When passed through a cation exchanger, the complex cation is exchanged, and the chloride ions pass into the eluted solution.

Molar mass of $AgCl = 108 + 35.5 = 143.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

Moles of $AgCl$ formed $= \dfrac{8.61}{143.5} = 0.06 \text{ mol}$

The ratio of moles of complex reacted to moles of $AgCl$ formed is:

$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.06} = \dfrac{1}{3} = 0.3333...$

This can be written as $33.33 \times 10^{-2}$.

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $33$.

Answer: $33$

question: Ammonia forms the complex ion $\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+}$ with copper ions in alkaline solutions but not in acidic solutions. What is the reason for it?
options: In acidic solutions protons coordinate with ammonia molecules forming $\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}$ions and $\mathrm{NH}_3$ molecules are not available
In alkaline solutions insoluble $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_2$ is precipitated which is soluble in excess of any alkali
Copper hydroxide is an amphoteric substance
In acidic solutions hydration protects copper ions.

q: The metal complex that is diamagnetic is (Atomic number : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>26</mn><mo>;</mo><mi>Cu</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>29</mn></math>)
question: The metal complex that is diamagnetic is (Atomic number : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>26</mn><mo>;</mo><mi>Cu</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>29</mn></math>)
solution:
K3CuCN4:

CuCN4-3

Oxidation number of of copper is Cu+1

Electronic configuration of Cu+1=Ar3 d104s0

Due to the absence of unpaired electrons, their spin only magnetic moment is zero, they are diamagnetic in nature

K2CuCN4:

CuCN42-, Cu2+=Ar3d9 one unpaired electron is present.Hence, paramagnetic in nature.

K4FeCl6:

FeCl6-4, Fe+2: Ar 3d6 4s0,one unpaired electron.Hence, paramagnetic in nature.

subject: Chemistry
q: The magnetic moment is measured in Bohr Magneton (BM). Spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> complexes respectively is:
question: The magnetic moment is measured in Bohr Magneton (BM). Spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> complexes respectively is:
solution:
The spin only magnetic moment can be calculated as follows

μ=nn+2BM

n=Number of unpaired electrons

FeH2O63+=d5(High spin complex)=5.unpaired electrons
μ=5×7=35
=5.92 B.M.
FeCN63+=d5(Low spin complex)=1
μ=1×3=3
=1.732 B.M.

question: The correct order of the spin –only magnetic moment of metal ions in the following low-spin complexes, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mrow><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mrow><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mrow><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> is:
solution:
(i)  V+223⟹Ar3d34d0⟹  n=3 ⇒μ33+2=3.87
(ii) Cr+224⟹Ar3d4 n=2 ⇒μ22+2=2.82
(iii)   Ru+344⟹kr4d5    (n=1) ⇒μ11+2=3
(iv)   Fe+226 ⟹Ar3d6  (n=0) ⇒μ=0
Order of magnetic moment will be ∝ no. of unpaired e-
V+2> Cr+2>Ru+3 >Fe+2
μ=n(n+2) B.M. Hence more the value of n more will be magnitude moment.
